sections.title: Forming the Conditional
sections.explanation: The conditional tense expresses what 'would' happen. It is formed using the same stem as the futur simple, but with the endings of the imparfait: -ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient. For regular -ER and -IR verbs, the stem is the full infinitive: parler → je parlerais (I would speak), finir → je finirais (I would finish). For regular -RE verbs, drop the final -e before adding the endings: vendre → je vendrais (I would sell). The same irregular stems used in the futur simple apply here: être → ser- (je serais), avoir → aur- (tu aurais), aller → ir- (il irait), faire → fer- (elle ferait), vouloir → voudr- (je voudrais), pouvoir → pourr- (tu pourrais), savoir → saur- (il saurait), venir → viendr- (je viendrais), voir → verr- (vous verriez), envoyer → enverr- (ils enverraient). All six endings follow the imparfait pattern: -ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient — so there is no need to learn new endings, only the stems.
sections.tip: The key formula is: FUTUR SIMPLE STEM + IMPARFAIT ENDINGS. If you know the futur simple (je parlerai), just swap the ending: je parlerAIS. The -ait ending is the most common singular third-person form you will see. For irregular verbs, the stem is always the same as in the futur simple — master those stems once and you have both tenses covered.

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct conditional form of parler for 'nous'?
exercises.explanation: The conditional is formed with the futur stem (parler-) plus the imparfait ending for nous (-ions): nous parlerions.

exercises.prompt: Il ___ (finir) ses devoirs avant le dîner. (He would finish his homework before dinner.)
exercises.hint: Futur stem of finir is finir- + ending -ait for il
exercises.explanation: For regular -IR verbs, the conditional stem is the infinitive: finir- + -ait = finirait.

exercises.prompt: What is the conditional of vendre for 'je'?
exercises.explanation: For -RE verbs, drop the final -e: vendr- + -ais = je vendrais.

exercises.prompt: Vous ___ (être) fatigués après le voyage. (You would be tired after the trip.)
exercises.hint: Être has an irregular stem: ser- + ending -iez for vous
exercises.explanation: Être is irregular: the conditional stem is ser-. With the vous ending -iez: seriez.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'He would go to Paris'?
exercises.explanation: Aller has the irregular conditional stem ir-: il + ir- + -ait = il irait.

exercises.prompt: Elles ___ (voir) le spectacle ensemble. (They would see the show together.)
exercises.hint: Voir has an irregular stem: verr- + ending -aient for elles
exercises.explanation: Voir is irregular: the conditional stem is verr-. With the elles ending -aient: verraient.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'We would be able to come' using the conditional of pouvoir?
exercises.explanation: Pouvoir has the irregular conditional stem pourr-. With the nous ending -ions: nous pourrions venir.

exercises.prompt: Tu ___ (savoir) la réponse. (You would know the answer.)
exercises.hint: Savoir has an irregular stem: saur- + ending -ais for tu
exercises.explanation: Savoir is irregular: the conditional stem is saur-. With the tu ending -ais: saurais.

sections.title: Uses of the Conditional
sections.explanation: The conditional has five main uses in French. (1) Polite requests — this is the most common use in everyday speech. Using voudrais instead of veux makes a request much more polite: Je voudrais un café (I would like a coffee), Pourriez-vous m'aider? (Could you help me?), Tu pourrais répéter? (Could you repeat?). (2) Hypothetical situations with si + imparfait in the condition clause and the conditional in the main clause: Si j'avais de l'argent, j'achèterais une voiture (If I had money, I would buy a car). IMPORTANT: never use the conditional after si — the imparfait always follows si. (3) Unverified or reported information (the journalistic conditional): Le président serait malade (The president is reportedly ill — unconfirmed). (4) Wishes and regrets: Je voudrais vivre à Paris, J'aimerais parler mieux français, Ce serait bien! (That would be nice!). (5) Advice with devoir: Tu devrais étudier plus (You should study more), Vous devriez partir (You should leave).
sections.tip: The two most important uses for everyday French are polite requests (Je voudrais, Pourriez-vous, Tu pourrais) and hypothetical si + imparfait sentences. Remember the golden rule: NEVER put the conditional after si — use the imparfait there instead. Think of it as: si-clause = imparfait, result clause = conditional.

examples.fr: Je voudrais un café, s'il vous plaît.
examples.en: I would like a coffee, please.

examples.fr: Si j'avais de l'argent, j'achèterais une voiture.
examples.en: If I had money, I would buy a car.
If she could, she would come.

exercises.prompt: You want to politely order a glass of water. Which sentence is most appropriate?
exercises.explanation: Je voudrais (conditional of vouloir) is the polite way to make a request. Je veux sounds abrupt; je voulais is the imparfait and doesn't convey a current request.

exercises.prompt: Si nous avions le temps, nous ___ (voyager) en Italie. (If we had the time, we would travel to Italy.)
exercises.hint: The main clause of a si + imparfait sentence uses the conditional: voyager- + -ions
exercises.explanation: In a si + imparfait hypothetical, the result clause takes the conditional: nous voyagerions.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the si + imparfait structure?
options: Si tu venais, ce serait super.
Si tu viendrais, ce serait super.
Si tu viens, ce serait super.
exercises.explanation: After si in a hypothetical, use the imparfait (venais), never the conditional (viendrais). The result clause then takes the conditional (serait).

exercises.prompt: ___ (pouvoir)-vous m'indiquer la gare? (Could you point me to the train station?)
exercises.hint: Polite request using the conditional of pouvoir for vous, inverted
exercises.explanation: Pourriez-vous is the polite inverted conditional form: pourr- (irregular stem) + -iez (vous ending).

exercises.prompt: A journalist writes: 'Il y ___ eu un accident.' What does this conditional convey?
exercises.explanation: Il y aurait eu — the journalistic conditional signals that the information is unconfirmed or reported, not directly witnessed by the speaker.

exercises.prompt: Tu ___ (devoir) dormir plus tôt. (You should go to sleep earlier.)
exercises.hint: Use the conditional of devoir for tu to give advice
exercises.explanation: Devoir in the conditional expresses advice or recommendation: tu devrais = you should.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I would love to speak French better'?
exercises.explanation: J'aimerais is the conditional of aimer, expressing a wish. J'aimais is the imparfait (I used to like), and j'aimerai is the futur simple (I will like).

exercises.prompt: Si elle ___ (avoir) plus de temps, elle lirait davantage. (If she had more time, she would read more.)
exercises.hint: After si in a hypothetical sentence, use the imparfait, not the conditional
exercises.explanation: The si-clause requires the imparfait: si elle avait. The conditional (lirait) goes in the result clause, never after si.
